data.title: Lucid Stock Slips As Company Holds Investor Day And Strategic Discussion
data.description:
Lucid Group Inc (NASDAQ:LCID) shares are down Thursday as investors weighed concerns over losses and potential share dilution.

At its Investor Day on March 12, Lucid outlined its strategy for growth and profitability. A key moment was a fireside chat between Interim CEO Marc Winterhoff and Uber President Andrew Macdonald, discussing their upcoming robotaxi partnership expected to launch later this year.

Neward, California-based Lucid also announced that Gravity owners in North America will receive an over-the-air software update starting March 12, enabling Apple CarPlay and Android Auto integration. The update will extend to owners in the Middle East and Europe later this month.

Despite these updates, investors remain cautious after Lucid posted a wider-than-expected loss of $3.08 per share in Q4, despite beating revenue estimates with $522.73 million. Concerns were also raised by the company's filing to resell up to 69.12 million shares linked to obligations with Uber and Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund.

Broader market weakness added to the pressure, with the S&P 500 down 0.8% and the Consumer Discretionary sector falling 0.9%.

Short Sellers Pile On To Lucid

Short interest in Lucid ticked up in the latest reporting period, rising from 44.08 million to 44.30 million shares — representing 52.89% of publicly available shares sold short, with shorts needing roughly 6.9 days to cover at current trading volumes.

Lucid Technical Analysis

Over the past 12 months, shares have decreased by 54.63%, and they are currently positioned closer to their 52-week lows than highs.

The RSI is at 54.75, which is considered neutral territory, suggesting that the stock is neither overbought nor oversold. Meanwhile, MACD is at -0.0922, with the signal line at -0.1961, indicating a bullish momentum as the MACD is above the signal line.

The combination of a neutral RSI and a bullish MACD suggests mixed momentum, indicating that while there may be some buying interest, the stock is still struggling to gain upward momentum.

  • Key Resistance: $10.50
  • Key Support: $9.50

Analyst Consensus & Recent Actions

The stock carries a Neutral Rating with an average price target of $8.54. Recent analyst moves include:

  • Cantor Fitzgerald: Neutral (Lowers Target to $14.00) (Feb. 25)
  • Morgan Stanley: Downgraded to Underweight (Lowers Target to $10.00) (Dec. 8, 2025)
  • Stifel: Hold (Lowers Target to $17.00) (Nov. 17, 2025)

Top ETF Exposure

  • SPDR S&P Kensho Smart Mobility ETF (NYSE:HAIL): 2.02% Weight
  • GraniteShares 2x Long LCID Daily ETF (NASDAQ:LCDL): 200.10% Weight

Significance: Because LCID carries such a heavy weight in these funds, any significant inflows or outflows will likely trigger automatic buying or selling of the stock.

LCID Stock Price Activity: Lucid Group shares were down 6.32% at $10.01 at the time of publication on Thursday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Image via Shutterstock

data.title: Buckle Up DraftKings, Flutter: CFTC Chairman Selig Has Good News For Prediction Markets
data.description:
CFTC Chairman Michael Selig went on CNBC’s Squawk Box Thursday morning and said what the sportsbook industry has been dreading: the definition of a commodity under the Commodity Exchange Act is “very broad.”

Selig elaborated that it includes “virtually any underlying that you might trade a derivative on” and that the CFTC does not “discriminate between sports and politics and corn.”

The statement is the clearest signal yet that the CFTC views sports event contracts as federally regulated swaps, not state-regulated gambling.

What Selig Said

Selig drew a sharp line between prediction market exchanges and sportsbooks. If a swap is structured on a commodity and traded on a CFTC-registered exchange, “that is not treated as gambling,” he said.

Sportsbooks, by contrast, offer no order book, no clearing house, and no ability to exit a position.

“Your transaction is with a bookie and not another person on the order book,” Selig told CNBC.

In an interview on the All-In Podcast Wednesday, Selig called prediction markets “truth machines” and said the CFTC has been policing fraud and manipulation in them since the Iowa Electronic Markets launched in the 1990s.

He pushed back on the idea that these markets are unregulated, pointing to Kalshi’s recent insider trading enforcement against a MrBeast employee who traded on non-public information about video launches.

Why It Matters For Your Portfolio

DKNG is down 30% year-to-date and is trading around $26 today. FLUT has fallen 50% in 2026, and is trading around $110.

Both stocks dropped earlier this week after Kalshi and Polymarket reportedly entered fundraising talks at $20 billion valuations each, roughly doubling their late-2025 figures.

Sports betting accounted for 80% of Kalshi’s all-time notional volume, according to Dune Analytics.

The legal picture is still messy.

An Ohio federal judge ruled against Kalshi this week, finding the CFTC does not have exclusive jurisdiction over sports contracts.

A Tennessee court reached the opposite conclusion in February.

More state-level lawsuits remain active, but Selig’s comments suggest the CFTC will keep fighting for federal control, and if that view holds in court, prediction markets may be able to legally offer sports betting nationwide without a single state license.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Campbell's Posts Q2 Miss On Weather, Other Factors
data.description:
Much of Campbell’s Co (NASDAQ:CPB) second-quarter sales and earnings miss was due to adverse weather in January, according to Bank of America Securities analyst Brian Holland.

The Campbell’s Analyst: Holland reaffirmed a Neutral rating and price target of $30.

The Campbell’s Thesis: The company's net sales declined by 4.5% year-on-year to $2,564 million and missed Street expectations of $2,617 million, Holland said in the note.

Check out other analyst stock ratings.

Campbell’s non-GAAP net income of 51 cents per share fell short of the consensus of 57 cents per share, he added.

Weather was not the only negative impact on the company's results, the analyst stated. Gross margin contracted by 270 basis points (bps) to 27.7%, due to factors like cost inflation, tariffs, and "unfavorable volume / mix," he further stated.

"At first blush, the FY26 guidance cut appears a more appropriate floor, which may ultimately help sentiment — but not today," Holland wrote.

CPB Price Action: Shares of Campbell’s had declined by 1.83% to $38.82 at the time of publication on Thursday.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Billionaire Charlie Munger Told A 30-Year-Old That Investing For 'Financial Freedom' Is Nearly Impossible, Unless You've Got $10 Million Saved
data.description:
Saving money is simple in theory. Spend less, invest the rest, and let time do the heavy lifting. That formula has powered countless retirement plans and personal finance books. 

But late Berkshire Hathaway Vice Chair Charlie Munger had a habit of poking holes in tidy investing narratives, especially when young investors assumed the stock market would make them wealthy.

During the Daily Journal annual shareholder meeting in 2015, Munger fielded a question from a 30-year-old attendee who wanted to know how someone his age could achieve financial freedom through investing. Munger's answer was blunt and grounded in decades of market experience.

Why Munger Thought Easy Investing Returns Were Fading

"Achieving success through investments has been pretty easy in my lifetime," Munger said.

For much of the 20th century, the math worked in investors' favor. The broad market delivered strong long term returns, and disciplined savers who consistently invested often saw their wealth compound steadily. That environment created a belief that investing alone could build financial independence.

Trending: Most founders obsess over the wrong hires. See the 5 startup roles that actually determine whether a company scales or stalls.

Munger warned that the future might not look the same.

"Now, if the world is going to get 10% out of indexes in the future, and I don't think it will, in real terms, getting more has proven to be quite difficult," he said. "Some of you who come along later are finding that if you stay in the big stocks, it's damn near impossible for most people."

Munger's message was not that investing was useless. It was that expecting exceptional returns had become far harder as markets grew larger and more efficient.

"When things are damn near impossible, maybe you could stop trying," Munger told the crowd.

The remark drew laughs, but the point was serious. In Munger's view, chasing unrealistic returns often leads investors into speculation, leverage, or trendy ideas that carry more risk than reward.

The $10 Million Joke That Carried a Lesson

Daily Journal CEO Gerry Salzman chimed in immediately after Munger finished his response, adding a note to the exchange. "Charlie says the way to get rich is to keep $10 million in your checking account in case a good deal comes along," Salzman said.

Munger quickly clarified the origin of the quote.

"By the way, that was the advice of Howard Ahmanson to a young bunch of starving graduates," Munger said. "Rich people sometimes get a little pompous."

The exchange got a laugh from the room, yet the underlying lesson was clear. Major opportunities rarely appear on a predictable schedule. Investors who want to act when rare bargains show up need both patience and available capital.

Why Munger's Warning Still Resonates in 2026

More than a decade after that Daily Journal meeting, Munger's comments still echo through today's investing environment. Markets remain heavily influenced by large technology companies, and professional investors, algorithms, and global funds compete to uncover every possible edge.

That competition makes it increasingly difficult for individual investors to consistently outperform the market.

Munger also cautioned against the idea that simply studying famous investors would unlock wealth.

"That was not my system, but I do not recommend my system to everybody," he said. "I do, as a way of life, but I don't think all you have to do is read Charlie Munger and you'll get rich. If it were that easy, this place would be a football stadium."

For investors navigating markets in 2026, the takeaway remains simple. Wealth rarely comes from shortcuts. It usually comes from disciplined saving, patience, and realistic expectations about what markets can deliver.

Image: Shutterstock
